content.problemStatement:
SudokuX is a variation of the popular game Sudoku. Similar to standard Sudoku, in SudokuX, we have to enter the numbers 1 - 9 once in each row, column and 3 x 3 square within the 9x9 puzzle grid. In addition to standard Sudoku, the numbers must only occur **once in each of the two diagonals**.

Johnny is very interested in playing SudokuX. He believes that this game is so challenging that even the best computer programs could not solve it efficiently.

Let's show Johnny the power of computers by writing a program to solve even the hardest SudokuX puzzles within seconds!
constraints: Each given SudokuX puzzle has a unique solution
content.inputFormat: The first line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of 9 lines. Each line contains 9 characters '.' (blank grid), or '1'..'9' representing a SudokuX puzzle. Each test case is separated by a blank line.
content.outputFormat: For each puzzle, output the solution in the same format as the input. Print a blank line after each test case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The given SudokuX puzzles are solved using the backtracking algorithm.
The given SudokuX puzzle is solved using the backtracking algorithm.
The given SudokuX puzzle is solved using the backtracking algorithm.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a backtracking algorithm. The algorithm tries to fill in the SudokuX puzzle by iterating over each empty cell and trying to place a number from 1 to 9 in it. If the number is valid (i.e., it does not appear in the same row, column, or 3x3 sub-grid, and it does not appear in the same diagonal), the algorithm recursively tries to fill in the rest of the puzzle. If it cannot find a valid number to place in the current cell, it backtracks and tries a different number in the previous cell.
solution.timeComplexity: O(9^(T*81)) per test case, O(T*9^(T*81)) total. The time complexity is exponential because in the worst case, the algorithm has to try all possible numbers for each cell.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(T*81) — the dominant structure is the SudokuX puzzle itself.
